content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)'. Demographic decline through 2040 will force school-network consolidation and closure of small selective tracks independent of policy choice, but government's simultaneous use of score-threshold levers is accelerating selective-track contraction beyond what demographic decline alone would cause, concentrating remaining grammar-school slots among already-advantaged urban populations.
opportunities: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
strengths: Annual births in Hungary fell from approximately 125,000 in the early 1990s to 77,500 in 2024, with a further 7.1% decline to ~72,000 in 2025, representing the lowest recorded level since 1949 and forcing school-network consolidation decisions in the 2020s–2030s regardless of selection policy.

Primary general school (általános iskola) enrollment declined 17.3% over 20 years, from 859,000 pupils in 2005/2006 to 710,000 in 2024/2025, tracking the shrinking birth cohorts directly, with first-grade intake falling 1.9% year-on-year in 2024/2025 to 95,000.
threats: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.
weaknesses: The net causal effect of early selection (6/8-year grammar-school entry at age 10–12) on individual student achievement and social mobility is not directly measured in Hungarian evidence; test-score gaps at selection reflect composition, not school value-added.

The relationship between consolidation-induced school closure, teacher workforce redeployment, and pedagogical quality in surviving small schools is unknown; closing small grammar schools may displace experienced teachers or may improve efficiency.
